title: Chef and Digits of Large Numbers

content.problemStatement:
Chef and Abhishek are two friends. Abhishek really appreciates Chef's skills and wants to learn the secret formula behind Chef's tasty dishes. Chef will tell his secret formula only to a
competent person. So to check Abhishek's competency, Chef asked him to solve the following problem.

Find the number of integers between 000 and NNN (inclusive) such that the product of digits of the integer is greater than or equal to K!K!K!, where KKK is the number of digits present in the integer.

**Example**: Consider N=1000N = 1000N=1000.


The only digit present in the integer 000 is 000. So the product of digit is also 000 and 1!=11! = 11!=1. So 000 will be not counted in the answer because 0≯10 \ngtr 10≯1.
The digits present in the integer 252525 are 2,52, 52,5. So the product of digits is (2⋅5)=10(2 \cdot 5) = 10(2⋅5)=10 and 2!=22 ! = 22!=2. So 252525 will be counted in the answer because  10≥210 \geq 210≥2.
The digits present in the integer 111111 are 1,11, 11,1. So the product of digits is (1⋅1)=1(1 \cdot 1) = 1(1⋅1)=1 and 2!=22 ! = 22!=2. So 111111 will be not counted in the answer because 1≯21 \ngtr 21≯2.
The digits present in the integer 157157157 are 1,5,71, 5, 71,5,7. So the product of digits is (1⋅5⋅7)=35(1 \cdot 5 \cdot 7) = 35(1⋅5⋅7)=35 and  3!=63! = 63!=6. So 157157157 will be counted in the answer because 35≥635 \geq 635≥6.
Since Abhishek is a lazy person, he wants your help to solve the problem.

**Note**: K!K!K! represents the factorial of KKK and K!=1⋅2⋅3⋅…⋅(K−1)⋅KK! = 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot \ldots \cdot (K-1) \cdot KK!=1⋅2⋅3⋅…⋅(K−1)⋅K .


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the answer for given integer NNN

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The only one-digit number that will not be included in the answer is 000. The two-digit numbers that will not be included in the answer are 10,1110, 1110,11 because their product of digits is less than 2!=22! = 22!=2. So the answer is =14−1−2=11= 14 - 1 - 2 = 11=14−1−2=11.
Test case 333:

The one-digit number that will not be counted in the answer is 000.
The two-digit digit numbers that will not be counted in the answer are 10,1110, 1110,11, 20,3020, 3020,30, 40,50,60,40, 50, 60,40,50,60, 70,80,9070, 80, 9070,80,90 because their product of digits is less than 2!=22! = 22!=2.
The three-digit numbers that will not be counted in the answer are 100100100, 101,102,101, 102,101,102, 103,104,105103, 104, 105103,104,105, 106,107,108106, 107, 108106,107,108, 109,110,111,109, 110, 111,109,110,111, 112,113,114,112, 113, 114,112,113,114, 115,120115, 120115,120,121, 121,121, 122,130,131,122, 130, 131,122,130,131, 140,141,150,140, 141,      
150,140,141,150, 151,160,170151, 160, 170151,160,170, 180,190180, 190180,190 because their product of digits is less than 3!=63! = 63!=6.
So the answer is =199−1−10−29=159= 199 - 1 - 10 - 29 = 159=199−1−10−29=159.
